claim: The Voynich glyphs encode a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin, where the 25 glyphs map to a Latin alphabet expanded with homophones for high-frequency letters (e, a, i, t, s), reducing glyph entropy below the expected Latin ~4.0 bits to the observed 3.8627 bits.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is slightly below Latin ~4.0 bits, consistent with homophonic expansion flattening the frequency distribution
Word-initial constraint set {o,c,q,s,d} and word-final set {y,n,l,r,o} could reflect Latin morphological endings masked by homophones
Multiple high-confidence beliefs already support a homophonic cipher framework
Currier A/B cipher-table divergence is consistent with two homophonic tables for the same plaintext
evidence_against: Hapax ratio 70.1% is far higher than expected for homophonic Latin, which should reduce not increase type diversity
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is lower than typical natural-language Latin (~1.0), suggesting either non-Latin or added noise
Word entropy 10.4508 bits exceeds typical enciphered Latin word distributions
test: Compute the bigram entropy and conditional entropy H(G2|G1) for all adjacent glyph pairs; compare to theoretical homophonic Latin bigram structure. Under homophonic substitution on Latin, H(G2|G1) should be lower than the unconditioned glyph entropy (3.8627) and should match shuffled Latin bigram profiles within 0.05 bits.
test_metric: Conditional bigram entropy H(G2|G1) across full corpus and separately for Currier A and B sub-corpora
test_threshold: Confirm if H(G2|G1) < 3.6 bits and the ratio H(G2|G1)/H(G1) falls in [0.88, 0.95], matching Latin homophonic profiles; deny if H(G2|G1) >= 3.8 bits (no redundancy exploited by bigrams)

reasoning: A homophonic cipher on Latin would produce the observed entropy suppression and glyph-positional patterns while accommodating the Currier A/B split as two distinct homophonic tables. Measuring conditional bigram entropy directly tests whether sequential glyph pairs carry the correlation structure expected from an underlying inflected language.
